title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marine biologist called Dr. Thomas Field talking about his work restoring coral reefs. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marine biologist called Dr. Thomas Field talking about his work restoring coral reefs. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Dr. Thomas Field discusses the innovative techniques used to regrow damaged coral ecosystems.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. My name is Dr. Thomas Field and I’ve spent the last fifteen years underwater—well, at least it feels that way. I’m a specialist in coral reef restoration. [7]I first became passionate about the ocean after completing my degree in marine biology at Bristol University[/7]. It was there that I realized how fragile our underwater ecosystems really are. Coral reefs are often called the 'rainforests of the sea', but they are dying at an alarming rate. One of our main tasks is 'coral gardening'. [8]We found that the overgrowth of algae was the most significant threat to the young coral fragments in our nursery[/8]. We have to clean them manually almost every day. It’s tedious but necessary. We also use some cutting-edge technology. [9]We’ve been experimenting with the use of low-voltage electricity to accelerate the growth of the coral’s limestone skeleton[/9]. It’s a process called Biorock technology, and the results have been staggering—corals grow up to five times faster. Another innovation is structural. [10]We are now using 3D printing to create complex artificial reef structures that mimic natural formations[/10]. This provides an immediate home for fish while the real coral grows over it. Working at sea is always unpredictable. [11]I was surprised to find that the frequency of heavy storms was the biggest challenge for our offshore teams[/11], as they can destroy months of work in a single night. Despite this, we have strong local support. [12]Collaborating with local fishermen has been a vital part of the project’s success[/12]. They know the waters better than anyone and have become our best conservationists. Looking to the future, we are focusing on resilience. [13]I believe that increasing genetic diversity is the only way reefs will survive rising ocean temperatures[/13]. We are breeding 'super corals' that can handle the heat. As we often say in the field, [14]we are not just planting coral, we are acting as 'reef architects' for the next generation[/14]. It’s a race against time, but one we intend to win.

dialogue.speaker: thomas
dialogue.text: Good morning. My name is Dr. Thomas Field and I’ve spent the last fifteen years underwater—well, at least it feels that way. I’m a specialist in coral reef restoration. I first became passionate about the ocean after completing my degree in marine biology at Bristol University. It was there that I realized how fragile our underwater ecosystems really are. Coral reefs are often called the 'rainforests of the sea', but they are dying at an alarming rate. One of our main tasks is 'coral gardening'. We found that the overgrowth of algae was the most significant threat to the young coral fragments in our nursery. We have to clean them manually almost every day. It’s tedious but necessary. We also use some cutting-edge technology. We’ve been experimenting with the use of low-voltage electricity to accelerate the growth of the coral’s limestone skeleton. It’s a process called Biorock technology, and the results have been staggering—corals grow up to five times faster. Another innovation is structural. We are now using 3D printing to create complex artificial reef structures that mimic natural formations. This provides an immediate home for fish while the real coral grows over it. Working at sea is always unpredictable. I was surprised to find that the frequency of heavy storms was the biggest challenge for our offshore teams, as they can destroy months of work in a single night. Despite this, we have strong local support. Collaborating with local fishermen has been a vital part of the project’s success. They know the waters better than anyone and have become our best conservationists. Looking to the future, we are focusing on resilience. I believe that increasing genetic diversity is the only way reefs will survive rising ocean temperatures. We are breeding 'super corals' that can handle the heat. As we often say in the field, we are not just planting coral, we are acting as 'reef architects' for the next generation. It’s a race against time, but one we intend to win.

questions.question: Thomas says his commitment to conservation began while he was studying (7) .
questions.explanation: He mentions completing his degree in marine biology at Bristol University.

questions.question: According to Thomas, the presence of (8) is the main danger to coral in the early stages of growth.
questions.explanation: He states the overgrowth of algae was the most significant threat to young coral.

questions.question: The team uses (9) as a way to make the coral grow much more quickly than normal.
questions.explanation: They use low-voltage electricity to accelerate the growth of the limestone skeleton.

questions.question: Thomas mentions that (10) is being used to build the foundations for new reefs.
questions.explanation: He says they are using 3D printing to create artificial reef structures.

questions.question: Thomas admits that (11) often cause unexpected delays and damage to their work.
questions.explanation: He was surprised that the frequency of heavy storms was the biggest challenge.

questions.question: Thomas was pleased to receive help from (12) during the restoration process.
questions.explanation: Collaborating with local fishermen has been a vital part of the success.

questions.question: Thomas is convinced that (13) is necessary to ensure corals can withstand climate change.
questions.explanation: He believes increasing genetic diversity is the only way for reefs to survive.

questions.question: Thomas uses the term (14) to describe the creative nature of his team's work.
questions.explanation: He says they are acting as 'reef architects' for the next generation.
